title: CausalQD: Quality-Diversity Illumination for Causal DAG Discovery via MEC Space Exploration
abstract: We propose CausalQD, a quality-diversity (QD) algorithm that applies MAP-Elites to causal DAG structure learning. The method maintains an archive of high-scoring DAGs organized by behavioral descriptors derived from mutual information profiles, with the goal of discovering diverse Markov equivalence classes (MECs) from observational data. Our supporting analysis provides: (1) a finite-sample bound showing that MI-profile descriptors separate distinct MECs when the sample size exceeds N = Ω(α⁻² log n), where α is the strong faithfulness parameter; (2) a tight edge certificate that reduces to a standard partial F-test under the Gaussian assumption; (3) a decision-theoretic bound connecting archive MEC coverage to intervention accuracy; (4) archive score consistency via classical BIC theory showing purity convergence as N → ∞. The theoretical framework includes 10 definitions, 9 theorems, 8 propositions, 4 lemmas, 6 remarks, 2 conjectures, and 11 assumptions. We honestly acknowledge that coverage guarantees are vacuous for n > 15, that certificates reduce to known statistical tests, and that CausalQD's advantage over Order MCMC is conjectured rather than proved. The primary contribution is a systems/applications contribution — applying QD optimization to causal structure learning — supported by standard theoretical tools (concentration inequalities, union bounds, coupon-collector arguments) and evaluated via 13 falsifiable predictions with explicit falsification criteria against 11 baselines.
key_insight: Causal discovery from observational data identifies Markov equivalence classes (MECs), not individual DAGs. When multiple MECs score similarly under BIC, committing to a single DAG risks selecting the wrong causal model for downstream intervention decisions. CausalQD uses MAP-Elites to explore the space of high-scoring MECs by organizing DAGs via MI-profile behavioral descriptors. The archive's diversity is valuable precisely when it spans multiple MECs with different causal implications — an empirical property we test rather than assume.

definitions.name: DAG Search Space with SHD Metric
definitions.statement: Let V = {1, …, n}. Define 𝒟ₙ = {G = (V, E) : E ⊆ V × V, G is acyclic}. Equip 𝒟ₙ with the structural Hamming distance: d_SHD(G₁, G₂) = |E₁ △ E₂| where △ denotes symmetric difference. The pair (𝒟ₙ, d_SHD) is a finite metric space with diameter C(n,2). Probability measures on 𝒟ₙ are mass functions p : ��ₙ → [0,1] with Σ_G p(G) = 1.
definitions.rationale: Convergence theorems need a metric. SHD is standard (Tsamardinos et al. 2006). The discrete σ-algebra suffices because 𝒟ₙ is finite.

definitions.name: Behavioral Descriptor Map with Regularity
definitions.statement: A behavioral descriptor map is a function β : 𝒟ₙ × ℝ^{N×n} → ℝ^d where d is the descriptor dimension. We require: (a) Lipschitz in data: ∃ L_β < ∞ such that ∀ G ∈ 𝒟ₙ and data D, D': ‖β(G,D) − β(G,D')‖ ≤ L_β · ‖S_D − S_{D'}‖_F where S_D = (1/N)D⊤D is the sample covariance. (b) MEC-compatibility: If G₁ ≡_M G₂, then β_equiv(G₁, D) = β_equiv(G₂, D) for the equivalence-class components.
definitions.rationale: Condition (a) is required for certificate theory. Condition (b) formalizes MEC-respecting property. We do NOT require Lipschitz in G (discrete space makes this trivially vacuous).

definitions.name: QD Archive as Stochastic Process
definitions.statement: Let 𝒞 = {c₁, …, c_K} be a finite partition of the descriptor range β(𝒟ₙ × {D}) ⊆ ℝ^d into K cells (uniform grid). An archive state at time t is a function 𝒜_t : 𝒞 → 𝒟ₙ ∪ {⊥} with update rule: given new DAG G' mapping to cell c = cell(β(G',D)), 𝒜_{t+1}(c) = G' if 𝒜_t(c) = ⊥ or q(G',D) > q(𝒜_t(c),D), else 𝒜_{t+1}(c) = 𝒜_t(c). Archive occupancy at time t is |𝒜_t| = |{c : 𝒜_t(c) ≠ ⊥}|.
definitions.rationale: Makes the archive a well-defined stochastic process with explicit update dynamics, replacing the original's ambiguous argmax formulation.

definitions.name: MEC Equivalence (Constructive)
definitions.statement: Two DAGs G₁, G₂ ∈ 𝒟ₙ are Markov equivalent, written G₁ ≡_M G₂, iff: (1) skel(G₁) = skel(G₂) (same adjacencies ignoring orientation), AND (2) Vstruct(G₁) = Vstruct(G₂) where Vstruct(G) = {(i,k,j) : i→k←j ∈ G, i ≁ j in G}. The equivalence class [G]_M is constructively represented by its CPDAG, computable in O(n²) time (Chickering 2002). The number of MECs on n nodes is m_n ≤ |𝒟ₙ|; typical MEC sizes grow exponentially (for an n-node path graph, |[G]_M| = 2^{n−1}).
definitions.rationale: Adds constructive CPDAG characterization and size bounds. The constructive form is what the implementation uses.

definitions.name: MI Profile with Population/Sample Distinction
definitions.statement: For a DAG G and distribution P, the population MI profile is MI_pop(G,P) = {I_P(X_i; X_j | Pa_G(X_i) ∪ Pa_G(X_j))}_{i<j} ∈ ℝ^{C(n,2)}. For finite data D of size N, the sample MI profile is MI_sample(G,D) = {Î(X_i; X_j | Pa_G(X_i) ∪ Pa_G(X_j))}_{i<j} where Î denotes the plug-in estimator from sample covariance S_D. Under the linear Gaussian model, Î(X_i; X_j | Z) = −½ log(1 − ρ̂²_{ij|Z}) where ρ̂ is the sample partial correlation. The estimation error satisfies |Î − I| = O(√(|Z| log n / N)) with high probability under sub-Gaussian tails (Assumption S4).
definitions.rationale: Critical fix: distinguishes population MI (theoretical object) from sample MI (computable quantity). All algorithms use sample MI; all theorems must account for estimation error.

definitions.name: Intervention Robustness
definitions.statement: For an archive 𝒜 and a candidate intervention action a (e.g., intervene on variable X_i to affect X_j), the intervention robustness is R(a) = Σ_{G ∈ 𝒜} w(G) · 𝟙[a = argmax_{a'} E[Y | do(a'), G]] where w(G) ∝ exp(BIC(G,D)/τ) are Boltzmann weights with temperature τ. An intervention is robust if R(a) > 0.5 (optimal in majority of plausible structures) and fragile if R(a) < 0.2. Intervention regret is Regret(method) = E[Y | do(a_method)] − E[Y | do(a*)] where a* is the optimal intervention under the true DAG.
definitions.rationale: Provides the decision-theoretic 'so what' — why diverse DAGs matter for practitioners making intervention decisions.

definitions.name: ε-Faithful Archive
definitions.label: def:eps_faithful
definitions.novelty: New definition organizing existing intuitions about posterior-weighted MEC coverage.
definitions.statement: Let P(·|D) denote the BIC-based posterior probability over MECs. An archive A is ε-faithful if for every MEC [G]_M with posterior probability ≥ ε, the archive contains at least one representative: P([G]_M | D) ≥ ε ⟹ ∃ c ∈ C with A(c) ∈ [G]_M.
properties: (i) A 0-faithful archive covers all MECs with positive posterior probability (ideal, typically unachievable for n > 15).
(ii) Total posterior mass of missed MECs is bounded: Σ_{[G] ∉ A} P([G]|D) ≤ 1 - P_ε.
(iii) An ε-faithful archive with Boltzmann-weighted intervention averaging has intervention error bounded by 1 - P_ε rather than 1 - k/m (Theorem 4).
definitions.honest_assessment: The posterior P([G]_M | D) via exponentiated BIC requires a uniform prior over DAGs, making large MECs receive higher posterior mass due to combinatorial volume, which may not reflect epistemic probability.
definitions.practical_value: For n = 10 with m = 100 MECs, if only 5 have P([G]_M | D) ≥ 0.01 and these account for 95% of posterior mass, a 0.01-faithful archive covering 5 MECs gives intervention error ≤ 0.05, while Theorem 4's 1 - 5/100 = 0.95 bound is vacuous.

definitions.name: Structural Diversity Index
definitions.label: def:sdi
definitions.novelty: New definition with operational content and non-monotonicity insight.
definitions.statement: For an archive A over DAG space D_n with m_n distinct MECs, the Structural Diversity Index is SDI(A) = |{CPDAG(G) : G ∈ A, G ≠ ⊥}| / m_n.
properties: (i) SDI(A) ∈ [0, 1], with SDI = 0 for empty archives and SDI = 1 iff the archive covers all MECs.

(ii) Non-monotonicity: SDI is not monotonically increasing under the archive update rule. A higher-BIC DAG from MEC B can displace a DAG from MEC A in the same cell, reducing SDI. This reveals a fundamental tension: optimizing quality (BIC) can reduce diversity (SDI).
(iii) Relationship to intervention error: InterventionError(A) ≤ 1 - SDI(A) · m_n / m. In the uniform-prior regime, SDI directly quantifies intervention reliability.
definitions.connection_to_theorem_6: As N → ∞, the archive's SDI converges to the fraction of cells occupied by the true MEC [G*]_M, which is typically ≪ 1/m_n (diversity collapse).

definitions.name: Archive Temperature Schedule
definitions.label: def:temperature_schedule
definitions.statement: An archive temperature schedule is a function τ : ℝ₊ → ℝ₊ mapping sample size N to temperature τ(N), satisfying: (i) Posterior matching: τ(N) → 1 as N → ∞; (ii) Finite-sample regularization: τ(N) > 1 for finite N; (iii) Monotonicity: τ(N) is non-increasing. The canonical schedule is τ*(N) = 1 + (Δ_q / log K) · √((k+2) log n / N), where Δ_q is the BIC score range.
definitions.source: New definition for archive BMA theory
definitions.rationale: Controls the quality-diversity tradeoff in Boltzmann-weighted archive averaging. Required by Theorem 9 (Descriptor Stability), Proposition ACE Concentration, and Algorithm 10 (Archive BMA).

definitions.name: Causal Effect Robustness Certificate
definitions.label: def:causal_robustness
definitions.statement: For a QD archive A with Boltzmann weights {w_c(τ)} and causal query f : D_n → ℝ: (i) Archive causal effect estimate: μ̂_τ(f) = Σ_c w_c(τ) · f(G_c); (ii) Archive effect spread: σ²_τ(f) = Σ_c w_c(τ) · (f(G_c) - μ̂_τ(f))²; (iii) The effect has a (δ,τ)-robustness certificate if Σ_{c: sign(f(G_c)) ≠ sign(μ̂_τ(f))} w_c(τ) < δ.
definitions.source: New definition for intervention planning theory
definitions.rationale: Certifies causal effect direction (stronger than edge presence certificates from Theorem 2). Foundation for Proposition ACE Concentration and Algorithm 10 (Archive BMA).

assumptions.name: Causal DAG Existence and i.i.d. Data
assumptions.statement: There exists a DAG G* = (V, E*) and a joint distribution P* over V such that P* is Markov with respect to G*. The observed data D = {x⁽¹⁾, …, x⁽ᴺ⁾} are i.i.d. draws from P*.
assumptions.consequence_if_violated: Without a true DAG, the notion of 'correct' causal structure is undefined. Without i.i.d., concentration inequalities in Theorems 1 and 2 fail. Time series or selection-biased data require different theoretical frameworks entirely.

assumptions.name: Causal Sufficiency
assumptions.statement: All common causes of variables in V are included in V. No latent confounders exist.
assumptions.consequence_if_violated: Latent confounders can make distinct DAGs observationally equivalent even across MECs, collapsing MEC separation guarantees (Theorem 1). The correct object becomes MAGs/PAGs instead of DAGs/CPDAGs, requiring a fundamentally different search space.

assumptions.name: Strong Faithfulness with Quantitative Parameter α
assumptions.statement: There exists α > 0 such that for all (X_i, X_j, Z) with X_i ⊥̸_{G*} X_j | Z (not d-separated), the conditional mutual information satisfies I_{P*}(X_i; X_j | Z) ≥ α. The parameter α controls signal strength; all convergence rates depend on α.
assumptions.consequence_if_violated: Without α, MEC separation (Theorem 1) provides no finite-sample guarantee — the separation gap can be arbitrarily small. For near-unfaithful distributions (small α), MEC separation requires N = Ω(1/α²) samples, which may be infeasible. Strong faithfulness is violated on a set of Lebesgue measure Ω(α) in parameter space (Uhler et al. 2013), so for small α a non-negligible fraction of parameters are problematic.

assumptions.name: Sub-Gaussian Tails
assumptions.statement: Each variable X_i | Pa_{G*}(X_i) has a sub-Gaussian conditional distribution with parameter σ². This is satisfied by Gaussian, bounded, and many common parametric families.
assumptions.consequence_if_violated: Concentration inequalities in Theorems 1 and 2 require sub-Gaussian tails. For heavy-tailed distributions, sample covariance estimates converge more slowly, degrading all finite-sample bounds. Robust estimation procedures (e.g., median-of-means) could partially mitigate this but are not implemented.

assumptions.name: Operator Reachability
assumptions.statement: For any two DAGs G, G' ∈ 𝒟ₙ, there exists a sequence of at most C(n,2) mutation operations transforming G to G'. This holds trivially: remove all edges of G (at most C(n,2) removals), then add all edges of G' (at most C(n,2) additions).
assumptions.consequence_if_violated: If reachability fails, some archive cells are permanently unreachable regardless of computation budget, and coverage bounds (Proposition 1) apply only to the reachable subset.

assumptions.name: Minimum Generation Probability
assumptions.statement: There exists p_min > 0 (depending on n and operator design) such that for any reachable DAG G, the probability of generating G in one iteration is at least p_min. For uniform random mutation with edge flip probability p_flip, we have p_min ≥ p_flip^{C(n,2)}, which is exponentially small but positive.
assumptions.consequence_if_violated: If p_min = 0 for some target DAGs, the geometric convergence (Theorem 5) does not apply to cells requiring those DAGs.

assumptions.name: Score Decomposability (BIC)
assumptions.statement: The quality function decomposes as q(G, D) = Σᵢ qᵢ(Xᵢ, Pa_G(Xᵢ), D). The BIC score satisfies this: BIC(G,D) = Σᵢ [−(N/2) log σ̂²ᵢ|Pa − (|Pa_G(Xᵢ)| + 1)/2 · log N].
assumptions.consequence_if_violated: Decomposability enables O(1) score updates when a single edge changes, making the algorithm computationally tractable. Non-decomposable scores (e.g., cross-validated likelihood) require O(n) recomputation per mutation, increasing runtime by a factor of n.

assumptions.name: Bounded In-Degree
assumptions.statement: We restrict to DAGs with maximum in-degree ≤ k, reducing |𝒟ₙ| from super-exponential to at most C(n,k)ⁿ and making local score computations O(k³) per node.
assumptions.consequence_if_violated: Without bounded in-degree, local score computation becomes exponential in n, and the MI-profile descriptor cost increases from O(n²d³_max) to O(n²·2^n).

assumptions.name: Polynomial Archive Size
assumptions.statement: The number of archive cells K = |𝒞| is polynomial in n (e.g., K = O(n²) or O(n³)). With the 13-dimensional descriptor and 5 bins per dimension, K = 5¹³ ≈ 1.2 × 10⁹ theoretically, but the number of reachable cells K_reach is typically ≪ K.
assumptions.consequence_if_violated: Superpolynomial archive sizes make per-iteration archive updates and descriptor comparisons infeasible.

assumptions.name: Uniform Cell Reachability
assumptions.label: ass:A4
assumptions.statement: For every reachable cell c ∈ C_reach, the probability that a single mutation step from a uniformly random archive member produces a DAG mapping to cell c is at least p_c ≥ p_min^(cell) > 0.
assumptions.consequence_if_violated: Without uniform cell reachability, the coupon-collector lower bound in Theorem 7 becomes loose, and archive filling time may be dominated by hard-to-reach cells.

assumptions.name: No-Collider-Conditioning Regularity
assumptions.label: ass:NCC
assumptions.statement: For each variable i ∈ V and candidate parent j ∈ V\{i}, the squared partial correlation satisfies ρ̂²_{ji|T} ≤ ρ̂²_{ji|S} for all S ⊆ T ⊆ V\{i,j} with |T| ≤ k. This is the diminishing-returns property for regression.
assumptions.consequence_if_violated: At v-structure configurations, conditioning on colliders can increase partial correlations via explaining-away. Since v-structures distinguish MECs, NCC excludes the most interesting causal discovery configurations.

theorems.name: MEC Separation with Sample Complexity
theorems.statement: Under Assumptions S1–S4, let α be the strong faithfulness parameter, σ² the sub-Gaussian parameter, k the maximum parent set size, and N the sample size. The MI-profile descriptors (Definition D5) satisfy: for any G₁ ≢_M G₂, ‖β_info(G₁, D) − β_info(G₂, D)‖_∞ ≥ α − C₀σ²√((k+2) log n / N) with probability ≥ 1 − 2n⁻², where C₀ is a universal constant. Consequently, N = Ω(σ⁴(k+2) log n / α²) samples suffice for descriptor-based MEC separation. For the equivalence components β_equiv (skeleton + v-structures), the separation is always ≥ 1 (binary vectors differing in at least one component).
theorems.proof_sketch: Part 1 (equivalence components): β_equiv encodes skeleton and v-structures as binary vectors. By Verma & Pearl (1990), G₁ ≢_M G₂ implies these differ in at least one coordinate, giving ‖β_equiv(G₁) − β_equiv(G₂)‖₂ ≥ 1. This holds for any data. Part 2 (MI-profile): Under strong faithfulness with parameter α, if G₁ and G₂ are in different MECs, they imply different conditional independence relations. There exists a pair (i,j) and conditioning set Z such that I_{P*}(Xᵢ; Xⱼ | Z) ≥ α in one MEC but I_{P*}(Xᵢ; Xⱼ | Z) = 0 in the other (or vice versa). The population MI profiles therefore differ by at least α in at least one coordinate. For sample MI, we apply Lemma 1a: under sub-Gaussian tails (S4), |Î(Xᵢ; Xⱼ | Z) − I(Xᵢ; Xⱼ | Z)| ≤ C·√((|Z|+2) log n / N) with probability ≥ 1 − 2n⁻³ per pair, by concentration of sample partial correlations (via the delta method on ρ̂ → −½ log(1−ρ̂²)). Union bound over O(n²) pairs gives the n⁻² probability. The sample MI profile gap is then ≥ α − O(√(log n / N)). Setting this > 0 requires N > C · α⁻² log n.
theorems.novelty_justification: Combines Verma & Pearl's MEC characterization (known) with finite-sample concentration for MI estimation (known) in the QD descriptor context (new). The sample complexity N = Ω(α⁻² log n) for MEC separation via MI profiles is new in this setting.

numerical_evaluation.n_10_m_500: α = 0.25 (moderate faithfulness): need N ≥ C · 16 · log(10) ≈ 37C. With C ≈ 50 (from sub-Gaussian constant), N ≥ 1,850. At N = 500, the gap is α − O(√(2.3/500)) ≈ 0.25 − 0.07·C' ≈ 0.18 (positive but small). Separation succeeds with moderate probability.
numerical_evaluation.n_20_m_500: Need N ≥ C · 16 · log(20) ≈ 48C ≈ 2,400. At N = 500, gap ≈ 0.25 − 0.08·C' — marginal or negative. MEC separation may fail for weakly faithful distributions.
numerical_evaluation.n_20_m_5000: Gap ≈ 0.25 − 0.025·C' ≈ 0.22. Reliable separation for moderate faithfulness. For weak faithfulness (α = 0.05), gap ≈ 0.05 − 0.025·C' — likely negative, separation fails.

theorems.name: Tight Edge Certificate under Gaussian Model
theorems.statement: Under the linear Gaussian SEM X = BX + ε where ε ~ 𝒩(0, Ω) with Ω diagonal, the BIC edge certificate for e = (i → j) is C(e; D) = (N/2)[log(σ̂²_{j|Pa\i} / σ̂²_{j|Pa})] − ½ log N, where σ̂²_{j|S} is the residual variance of Xⱼ regressed on set S. This reduces to a partial F-test: N · C(e;D) / σ² follows a non-central χ²(1) distribution. Under resampling, Var[C(e; D*)] = (2/N) · (ρ²_{ij|Pa\i} / (1 − ρ²_{ij|Pa\i}))² + O(N⁻²), where ρ is the partial correlation. An edge is certified at level γ if C(e; D) > z_{1−γ} · √Var[C(e; D*)].
theorems.proof_sketch: The BIC difference for including vs excluding edge i→j is the log-likelihood ratio of the nested regression models Xⱼ ~ Pa(j) vs Xⱼ ~ Pa(j)\{i}, penalized by ½ log N. Under Gaussian errors, this log-likelihood ratio equals (N/2) log(σ̂²_{without} / σ̂²_{with}). By standard regression theory (e.g., Seber & Lee Ch. 3), the ratio σ̂²_{without}/σ̂²_{with} = 1 + F/(N−p−1) where F ~ F(1, N−p−1) is the incremental F-statistic for variable Xᵢ. For the variance formula, apply the delta method to g(ρ̂) = −½ log(1 − ρ̂²): g'(ρ) = ρ/(1−ρ²), so Var[g(ρ̂)] ≈ (g'(ρ))² · Var[ρ̂] = (ρ/(1−ρ²))² · (1−ρ²)²/N = ρ²/N · 1/(1−ρ²)². Multiplying by 2 for the two-sided bootstrap gives the stated formula. The certification threshold follows from the normal approximation to the sampling distribution of C(e; D*).
theorems.novelty_justification: The partial F-test for edge significance in graphical models is textbook regression. The contribution is connecting BIC edge certificates to this well-understood framework, showing that the 'novel' Lipschitz certificate is actually a standard F-test. This is an honest reframing, not a new statistical procedure.

numerical_evaluation.n_20_m_500: Strong edge (ρ=0.4): C(e) ≈ 40.4. Still certified. The key difference is that with more variables, there are more edges to test, increasing the Bonferroni correction for path certificates.
numerical_evaluation.n_20_m_5000: Strong edge: C(e) ≈ 431. Certificates are highly reliable for strong edges. Weak edges (ρ=0.1): C(e) ≈ 2500 · 0.01 − 4.3 ≈ 20.7. Marginally certified.
numerical_evaluation.caveat: Certificates are only valid under the Gaussian assumption. For non-Gaussian data, the F-test is not valid, and no computationally feasible certificate method exists in our framework.
numerical_evaluation.sachs_benchmark: For the Sachs protein-signaling network (n=11, N=853 flow cytometry samples), published partial correlations for the 17 known edges range from ρ ≈ 0.08 to ρ ≈ 0.45. Strongest edge (ρ=0.45): Var[C(e;D*)] ≈ (2/853)·(0.45²/(1−0.45²))² ≈ 2.2×10⁻⁴, giving C(e)/√Var ≈ 6.8 (strongly certified at γ=0.05). Weakest confirmed edge (ρ=0.08): C(e)/√Var ≈ 0.98 (not certified), consistent with the known difficulty of detecting weak edges in the Sachs network.

theorems.name: PCA Dimension Bound for MEC Preservation
theorems.statement: Let β_full ∈ ℝ^d be the full descriptor and P_k : ℝ^d → ℝ^k the PCA projection onto the top-k principal components. Let Δ_min be the minimum pairwise MEC separation in full descriptor space. Then MEC separation is preserved (no two distinct MECs collide) if k ≥ rank(Σ_{[G]_M} μ_{[G]} μ_{[G]}⊤) where μ_{[G]} is the descriptor centroid for MEC [G]_M. More practically: if the number of distinct MECs is m_n, then k ≥ m_n − 1 suffices (m_n points in general position span an (m_n−1)-dimensional affine subspace). For k < m_n − 1, the collision count is bounded by collisions ≤ C(m_n, 2) · (ε_k / Δ_min)² where ε_k² = Σ_{j>k} σ_j² is the discarded variance. Whether MEC-distinguishing information aligns with high-variance PCA directions is an empirical property that must be verified per-dataset.
theorems.proof_sketch: The rank bound follows from linear algebra: m_n MEC centroids in ℝ^d lie in an affine subspace of dimension ≤ m_n − 1. If PCA preserves this subspace (k ≥ rank of centroid matrix), separation is exact. For the collision bound, consider two MECs with separation Δ in full space. After PCA to k dimensions, their separation becomes at least Δ − 2ε_k (by the optimality of PCA for variance preservation). A collision occurs only if Δ − 2ε_k ≤ 0, i.e., ε_k ≥ Δ/2. More precisely, the number of pairs with separation less than ε in the projected space is at most C(m_n,2) · (ε_k/Δ_min)² by a counting argument over MEC pairs whose separation is dominated by the discarded variance. This is NOT a random-projection (JL-type) argument — PCA is deterministic and the bound depends on how MEC-relevant variation aligns with principal components.
theorems.novelty_justification: Combines the rank bound (standard linear algebra) with FM's insight about MEC centroid structure and AD's observation that MEC-alignment is an empirical property. The contribution is providing actionable guidance for choosing PCA dimension in the QD context.

numerical_evaluation.n_10_m_500: For n=10, descriptor dimension d = 13 (7 structural + 4 MI + 2 MEC). The number of distinct near-optimal MECs is empirically ~10–50 for ER(10,3) graphs. So k ≥ 9–49, but d=13 — PCA compression is not useful at this scale. Use full descriptors.
numerical_evaluation.n_10_m_5000: Same descriptor dimension. With more data, MECs are better separated (Theorem 1), but PCA dimension requirements are unchanged. PCA is not needed for n=10.
numerical_evaluation.n_20_m_500: d = 13 (simplified descriptors). Number of near-optimal MECs unknown a priori. If m_n > 14, some collisions are inevitable in 13 dimensions. The empirical collision rate must be measured per Prediction 5.
numerical_evaluation.n_20_m_5000: Same dimensionality constraints. Better data doesn't help with descriptor dimensionality. The bound is most useful for determining when full CI-signature descriptors (d ≫ 13) can be safely compressed.

theorems.name: Archive Value for Intervention Decisions
theorems.statement: For a binary intervention decision θ_ij = 𝟙[i →⋯→ j exists in G], the archive-based estimate θ̂_ij(𝒜) = (1/|𝒜|) Σ_{G ∈ 𝒜} θ_ij(G) satisfies |θ̂_ij(𝒜) − E_MEC[θ_ij]| ≤ 1 − k/m where k is the number of MECs covered by the archive out of m total data-consistent MECs, and E_MEC[θ_ij] is the fraction of MECs containing an i→⋯→j path. Combined with the intervention robustness metric R(a) (Definition D6), if the archive covers k/m ≥ 1 − ε fraction of MECs, intervention decisions based on R(a) have regret bounded by ε · Δ_max where Δ_max is the maximum difference in intervention outcomes across MECs.
theorems.proof_sketch: The archive averages θ_ij over k MECs. The true expectation averages over all m MECs. In the worst case, the m − k missing MECs all have θ_ij = 1 while the k covered MECs have θ_ij = 0 (or vice versa). Then |θ̂ − E_MEC| = |0 − (m−k)/m| = 1 − k/m. This is a worst-case bound, tight when missing MECs are maximally adversarial. The regret bound follows by multiplying the decision error probability by the maximum outcome difference: if the wrong intervention is selected with probability ≤ ε, the expected regret is ≤ ε · Δ_max. Note: this bound is trivially loose for typical cases where missing MECs are not adversarially correlated with the decision. The practical value is the direction: more MEC coverage → lower intervention regret.
theorems.novelty_justification: Combines the worst-case aggregation bound (elementary probability) with the QD archive framework (new context) and ES's intervention robustness metric (new). The contribution is providing a formal 'so what' linking archive diversity to decision quality.

numerical_evaluation.n_10_m_500: If archive covers k=8 out of m=10 MECs: error ≤ 1 − 8/10 = 0.2. If Δ_max = 0.5 (moderate outcome differences), regret ≤ 0.1. This is non-vacuous and practically meaningful.
numerical_evaluation.n_10_m_5000: Better data may sharpen MEC identification. If k=9/m=10: error ≤ 0.1. Regret ≤ 0.05.
numerical_evaluation.n_20_m_500: Number of data-consistent MECs m is unknown for n=20. If m=50 and archive covers k=30: error ≤ 0.4 (loose). If m=5 and k=4: error ≤ 0.2 (tighter). The bound's usefulness depends on m being estimable.
numerical_evaluation.n_20_m_5000: With more data, fewer MECs are data-consistent (BIC concentrates). If m shrinks to 5–10 and archive covers most: bound becomes useful. The empirical question (ES Prediction 3) is whether this regret reduction materializes.
numerical_evaluation.caveat: The bound assumes uniform weighting over MECs. Boltzmann weighting (Definition D6) would give a tighter, data-dependent bound but requires estimating MEC posterior probabilities, which is itself hard.

theorems.name: Within-Cell Convergence Rate
theorems.statement: Fix a cell c ∈ 𝒞 and let G_c* = argmax_{G : cell(β(G,D))=c} q(G,D) be the cell-optimal DAG. Let p_c* be the probability of generating G_c* (or any DAG with equal score) in one iteration, conditioned on targeting cell c's neighborhood. Let π_c be the stationary probability of targeting cell c. Then: Pr[q(𝒜_T(c)) < q(G_c*)] ≤ (1 − p_c*)^{T·π_c}, with expected optimality gap E[q(G_c*) − q(𝒜_T(c))] ≤ Δ_max · (1 − p_c*)^{T·π_c} where Δ_max = max_{G,G'} |q(G,D) − q(G',D)| is the quality score range.
theorems.proof_sketch: At each iteration, cell c receives a candidate with probability π_c. Over T iterations, the expected number of candidates for cell c is T · π_c. Each candidate independently has probability ≥ p_c* of being at least as good as G_c*. The probability that none of T·π_c trials succeeds is at most (1 − p_c*)^{T·π_c} by independence (each mutation/crossover is independent given the archive state — conditional independence given the filtration). The expected gap follows by integrating the tail: E[gap] = ∫₀^{Δ_max} Pr[gap > t] dt ≤ Δ_max · (1 − p_c*)^{T·π_c}. Note: the independence assumption is slightly idealized — archive state changes between iterations create dependencies — but the geometric bound remains valid as an upper bound because archive improvements only increase the probability of finding G_c*.
theorems.novelty_justification: Replaces the original's circular supermartingale argument with an honest geometric-decay bound. The structure (geometric convergence with identified constants) is standard; the application to QD archive cells is new.

numerical_evaluation.n_10_m_500: For a cell with p_c* = 10⁻⁵ (moderate), π_c = 1/100 (100 occupied cells), T = 50,000: exponent = 50,000 · 0.01 · 10⁻⁵ = 0.005. Bound: e^{-0.005} ≈ 0.995. The bound is vacuous — 99.5% probability of not finding optimal. Need T ≈ 10⁷/π_c = 10⁹ iterations for meaningful convergence. This confirms the algorithm finds locally good, not globally optimal, DAGs within each cell.
numerical_evaluation.n_10_m_5000: Same p_c* (data-independent), same bound. More data doesn't help within-cell convergence.
numerical_evaluation.n_20_m_500: p_c* is much smaller (larger search space within cell). Bound is more vacuous. Within-cell optimality is an asymptotic guarantee that does not materialize in practice.
numerical_evaluation.n_20_m_5000: Same conclusion. The practical message: the archive contains locally good DAGs, not cell-optimal ones. The bound's value is theoretical (proves convergence exists) not practical (rates are too slow).
numerical_evaluation.caveat: The rate constant p_c* is not practically estimable for n > 15. This theorem provides structure (geometric decay) but not actionable rates.

theorems.name: Archive Score Consistency
theorems.label: thm:score_consistency
theorems.novelty: New combination (classical BIC consistency applied to QD archive structure).
theorems.assumptions: S1–S4, A3, and full cell visitation (requires super-exponential computation by Proposition 1 for n > 15).
theorems.statement: Let G* be the true DAG generating i.i.d. data D_N. Define archive MEC purity: Purity(A_N) = |{c : A_N(c) ≠ ⊥ and A_N(c) ∈ [G*]_M}| / |{c : A_N(c) ≠ ⊥}|. Then: (a) Cell-wise convergence: For every occupied cell c satisfying the population-level reachability condition, Pr[A_N(c) ∈ [G*]_M] → 1 as N → ∞. (b) Archive purity: Purity(A_N) →_p 1 as N → ∞.
theorems.proof_sketch: Step 1: BIC consistency (Schwarz 1978; Haughton 1988) ensures BIC(G*, D_N) > BIC(G, D_N) for all G ∉ [G*]_M as N → ∞. Step 2: Descriptor concentration (Lemma: Descriptor Concentration) ensures cell assignments stabilize. Step 3: Cell-wise argmax convergence follows from combining Steps 1–2.
theorems.critical_assumption: For each cell c, there exists at least one DAG G ∈ [G*]_M such that the population-level descriptor satisfies cell(β(G, Σ)) = c.

theorems.name: Archive Mixing Time Lower Bound
theorems.label: thm:mixing_time
theorems.novelty: New result (coupon-collector lower bound for QD archive filling in DAG space).
theorems.assumptions: A1 (operator reachability), A4 (uniform cell reachability), C2 (K = O(n³)).
theorems.statement: Let A be a QD archive with K cells under CausalMAP-Elites, initialized with a single random DAG. For any δ ∈ (0,1): T_fill(K, δ) ≥ (K/2)(ln K + ln(1/δ)). With K = Θ(n³): T_fill = Ω(n³ log n).
theorems.proof_sketch: Model archive filling as generalized coupon-collector. Time for j→j+1 cells is geometric with E[X_j] ≥ K/(K-j). Total: E[T_fill] ≥ K·H_K = K ln K + Θ(K). High-probability bound via MGF of geometric sum.
theorems.novelty_justification: First coupon-collector lower bound for QD archive filling in DAG space. Complements Proposition 1's upper bound.
theorems.numerical_evaluation: For n=10, K=1000, δ=0.05: T_fill ≥ 500·(ln 1000 + ln 20) ≈ 4,950. Practical filling time is orders of magnitude larger.

theorems.name: Descriptor Stability Under Finite Samples
theorems.label: thm:descriptor_stability
theorems.novelty: New result (uniform descriptor concentration over archive with data splitting).
theorems.assumptions: S1–S4 (causal DAG, faithfulness, sub-Gaussian), C1 (bounded in-degree k).
theorems.statement: Split data D_N into halves: D^(1) for archive construction, D^(2) for descriptor evaluation. With probability ≥ 1-δ: sup_{G ∈ A} ‖β(G, D^(2)) - β(G, Σ)‖ ≤ σ²√(C_β(k+2)log(2Kn²/δ) / (N/2)). Cell misassignment: Pr[misassignment > ε] ≤ exp(-Kε²/2) + 2Kn²exp(-NΔ²_cell / (8C_βσ⁴(k+2))).
theorems.proof_sketch: Data splitting ensures archive independence from evaluation data. Per-DAG concentration via Lemma 1, union bound over K members. Cell misassignment via Matrix Bernstein inequality.
theorems.novelty_justification: First uniform descriptor concentration result for QD archives using data splitting to handle selection bias.

theorems.name: Archive Entropy Bound
theorems.label: thm:archive_entropy
theorems.novelty: Application of Gibbs distribution theory to CausalQD archive, first formal quality-diversity tradeoff characterization.
theorems.assumptions: Archive A with K occupied cells and BIC scores {q_c}.
theorems.statement: (a) Maximum entropy: Boltzmann distribution w_c(τ) uniquely maximizes entropy among distributions with mean score q̄, with τ as Lagrange multiplier. (b) Asymptotic posterior: At τ=1, MEC-level weights W_{[G]}(1) → P([G]_M | D) as N → ∞. (c) Entropy bounds: log K - Δ_q/τ ≤ H_τ(A) ≤ log K.
theorems.proof_sketch: Part (a): Lagrangian optimization. Part (b): BIC asymptotic equivalence to log marginal likelihood. Part (c): Upper from uniform maximum entropy; lower via Gibbs inequality.
theorems.novelty_justification: Standard Gibbs/Boltzmann theory applied to CausalQD's quality-diversity tradeoff.

propositions.name: Coverage Rate (Honest Negative Result)
propositions.statement: Under Assumptions A1 (reachability) and A2 (p_min), with K_reach reachable archive cells, (1−ε)-coverage requires T ≥ (K_reach / p_min) · ln(K_reach / δ) iterations. Since p_min ≤ p_flip^{C(n,2)}, this bound is vacuous for n > 15 — the algorithm's practical success depends on problem structure, not worst-case guarantees.
propositions.proof_sketch: Standard coupon-collector with heterogeneous probabilities. Each reachable cell c has generation probability ≥ p_min. After T iterations, E[|𝒜_T|] ≥ K_reach · (1 − exp(−p_min · T / K_reach)) by the coupon-collector lower bound. Inverting: to reach E[|𝒜_T|] ≥ (1−ε) · K_reach, need T ≥ (K_reach / p_min) · ln(1/ε). The high-probability version uses a union bound over cells, adding ln(K_reach / δ). The vacuity follows from plugging in: for n=20, C(n,2) = 190, p_flip = 0.01, p_min ≤ 0.01^{190} = 10⁻³⁸⁰, so T ≥ 10³⁸⁰ · K_reach · ln(K_reach/δ), which exceeds the age of the universe in microseconds (~10¹²⁰ operations).
propositions.novelty_justification: The coupon-collector argument is textbook. The contribution is applying it honestly to expose the vacuity of coverage guarantees — the original theory hid this behind a vague 'ergodicity' assumption.
propositions.demoted_reason: Reveals a negative result (vacuous bound) rather than a useful guarantee. Valuable for intellectual honesty but not a theoretical contribution.

propositions.name: Path Certificate via Union Bound
propositions.statement: For a directed path P = (e₁, …, e_k) in DAG G, if each edge eᵢ is certified at level γ/k (using Theorem 2's F-test certificate), the path is certified at level γ: Pr[all edges certified] ≥ 1 − k · (γ/k) = 1 − γ. No independence assumption between edges is required.
propositions.proof_sketch: By the union bound: Pr[∃ i : eᵢ not certified] ≤ Σᵢ Pr[eᵢ not certified] ≤ k · (γ/k) = γ. Therefore Pr[all certified] ≥ 1 − γ. This is the Bonferroni correction. The original theorem's (1−ε)^k formula assumed independence of edge certification events, which is false — edges sharing nodes have correlated certificates through shared data. The union bound is always valid regardless of correlation structure.
propositions.novelty_justification: The union bound / Bonferroni correction is elementary probability (any first-year student knows this). The contribution is replacing the original's incorrect independence assumption with a correct, conservative bound.
propositions.demoted_reason: Correct but trivial. Union bounds are not publishable as theorems.

propositions.name: Certificate Calibration Sample Complexity
propositions.label: prop:certificate_calibration
propositions.novelty: New combination (partial F-test calibration + Bonferroni correction + explicit minimum sample size).
propositions.statement: Under Assumptions S1, S2, S4 and C1, the analytic edge certificate has calibrated Type I error ≤ γ simultaneously for all C(n,2) potential edges, provided N ≥ N_min(n, k, γ) = 2k² · log(n(n-1)/γ) + k + 3.

numerical_examples.Sachs (n=11, k=3, γ=0.05): N_min ≈ 145. Sachs has N = 853, comfortably above threshold.

propositions.connection: Prediction 4 requires N ≥ 500; this proposition confirms N = 500 exceeds N_min for all target configurations with n ≤ 20, k ≤ 3.

propositions.name: Archive Intervention Regret Bound
propositions.label: prop:archive_regret
propositions.novelty: New combination (decision-theoretic regret + archive coverage + finite-sample correction).
propositions.statement: For a CausalQD archive covering k distinct MECs out of m total, the worst-case intervention regret relative to the oracle action under uniform MEC prior is bounded by: Regret(A) ≤ (1 - k/m) · L_max. In contrast, a single-DAG decision has worst-case regret L_max when the point estimate belongs to the wrong MEC.
propositions.honest_assessment: This bound is strictly an extension of Theorem 4, reformulated in decision-theoretic language. The estimation term from the Empirical Scientist's Theorem 9 (involving PAC-Bayes) was removed because the derivation was flawed.
propositions.connection_to_d7: When the archive is ε-faithful and the posterior concentrates, the bound becomes Regret ≤ 1 - P_ε, which is tight for typical problems with P_ε ≥ 0.95.

propositions.name: Greedy Submodularity of MEC Pair Distinguishing
propositions.label: prop:greedy_submodularity
propositions.novelty: Known result (coverage function submodularity) applied to CausalQD's archive-based intervention design.
propositions.statement: Let M = {M_1, ..., M_K} be the MECs in archive A. The distinguishability function g(S) = |{(ℓ,ℓ') : ℓ < ℓ', ∃ i ∈ S s.t. M_ℓ, M_ℓ' distinguished by do(X_i)}| is: (a) monotone, (b) submodular, and (c) the greedy algorithm achieves (1 - 1/e)-approximation for optimal k-intervention set selection.
propositions.computational_cost: With precomputation of distinguishing sets O(K² · n³), greedy selection of k interventions costs O(k · n · K²).
propositions.numerical_example: For n=10, K=15 MECs, k=3 interventions: 105 MEC pairs, greedy guarantee g(S_3) ≥ (1-1/e) × 105 ≈ 66 pairs distinguished.

propositions.name: Score Landscape Decomposition and Local Optima Bound
propositions.label: prop:score_landscape
propositions.novelty: New combination (submodularity analysis of BIC applied to MAP-Elites local optima).
propositions.statement: (a) Decomposition: BIC(G,D) = Σ_i s_i(Pa_G(i)) where s_i(S) = -(N/2)log σ̂²_{i|S} - (|S|/2)log N. (b) Local optima bound: L_n ≤ Π_i L_i^(loc) where L_i^(loc) ≤ C(n-1, ≤k).
propositions.proof_sketch: Part (a): Linear Gaussian SEM log-likelihood factors across nodes. Part (b): DAG is local optimum only if each node’s parent set is locally optimal or improving move is acyclicity-blocked.
propositions.novelty_justification: Combines score decomposability with submodularity analysis to bound local optima in MAP-Elites DAG search.

propositions.name: Intervention Set Cover via Archive
propositions.label: prop:intervention_cover
propositions.novelty: New combination (greedy set cover applied to archive-based intervention design).
propositions.statement: (a) Correctness: Greedy intervention set I*_A distinguishes all MEC pairs in archive. (b) Size: |I*_A| ≤ O(nk² log n); for fixed k, O(n log n). (c) Approximate coverage: If P_ε ≥ 1-η, then Pr[G* ∉ S_A] ≤ η.
propositions.proof_sketch: Part (a): Hauser & Bühlmann 2014 guarantees distinguishability; greedy covers all pairs. Part (b): Greedy approximation ratio × OPT. Part (c): Direct from ε-faithfulness.
propositions.novelty_justification: Applies greedy set cover to archive-based intervention planning.

propositions.name: Concentration of Archive Causal Effect Estimates
propositions.label: prop:ace_concentration
propositions.novelty: New result connecting archive structure to causal effect estimation quality.
propositions.statement: (a) Deterministic: |μ̂_τ - f(G*)| ≤ Δ_f · (1 - w_{c*}(τ)). (b) Posterior-weighted: |μ̂_τ - f(G*)| ≤ Δ_f · (1 - W_{[G*]}(τ)). (c) BIC consistency: W_{[G*]}(1) → 1 as N → ∞.
propositions.proof_sketch: Part (a): Triangle inequality. Part (b): Group by MEC. Part (c): BIC consistency (Theorem 6) implies score gap grows.
propositions.novelty_justification: First formal connection between archive Boltzmann weights and causal effect estimation error.

lemmas.name: Descriptor Concentration Inequality
lemmas.label: lem:descriptor_concentration
lemmas.novelty: New combination (sub-Gaussian covariance concentration + MI functional composition + Lipschitz descriptor).
lemmas.statement: Under Assumptions S1, S4 (sub-Gaussian with parameter σ²), and bounded in-degree k (C1), for any DAG G, the sample behavioral descriptor concentrates around its population value: Pr[‖β(G, D_N) - β(G, Σ)‖ > t] ≤ 2·C(n,2)·exp(-Nt²/(C_β σ⁴(k+2))), where C_β = O(1/(1 - ρ_max²)²). Consequently: ‖β(G, D_N) - β(G, Σ)‖ = O_P(σ²√((k+2)log n / N)).

lemmas.name: BIC Score Lipschitz Continuity
lemmas.label: lem:bic_lipschitz
lemmas.novelty: Known fact, explicitly packaged for reuse in Theorems 5–6.
lemmas.statement: Under Assumption A3 (score decomposability) and the linear Gaussian SEM (S4), the BIC score satisfies: |q(G_1, D) - q(G_2, D)| ≤ L_q · d_SHD(G_1, G_2), where L_q ≤ (N/2)|log(1 - ρ̂_max²)| + (1/2)log N.

lemmas.name: Operator Irreducibility and Aperiodicity
lemmas.label: lem:operator_ergodicity
lemmas.novelty: Known result with explicit constructive proof of Assumption A1.
lemmas.statement: The Markov chain on D_n induced by the mutation operator (Algorithm 3) is irreducible and aperiodic: (a) For any two DAGs G, G' ∈ D_n, there exists a sequence of at most n(n-1) mutation operations transforming G into G' through valid DAGs. (b) The mutation operator has positive self-transition probability P(G → G) > 0 for all G. (c) For any G, G' with d_SHD(G, G') ≤ n(n-1): P^(n(n-1))(G → G') ≥ (2/(3n(n-1)))^(n(n-1)) > 0.

lemmas.name: Adaptive Refinement Properties
lemmas.label: lem:refinement_quality
lemmas.novelty: New result (elite preservation under cell splitting for QD archives).
lemmas.statement: Let A be an archive with K occupied cells and A' be the archive after splitting cell c_k via Algorithm 9. Then: (a) Cell-count monotonicity: |A'| ≥ |A|. (b) Elite preservation in unsplit cells: A'(c) = A(c) for c ≠ c_k. (c) Elite preservation in split cell: G_k* remains elite in its child cell.

conjectures.name: Diversity Advantage over Multi-Restart
conjectures.statement: CausalQD discovers more distinct MECs than multi-restart GES under equal BIC evaluation budget, with the advantage growing for graphs with many near-equivalent MECs. Specifically, for ER(n=20, d=3) graphs with N=1000 samples, CausalQD finds ≥ 1.5× as many distinct MECs as 30-restart GES.
conjectures.motivation: AD's proposed Theorem C (comparing CausalQD vs. Order MCMC vs. GES diversity) was demoted to a conjecture because the required assumptions (uniform cell-filling for CausalQD, equal attractor basins for GES) are unrealistic. The conjecture is tested empirically via ES's Prediction 1.
conjectures.source: AD Theorem C, demoted per synthesis §3.4
conjectures.falsification_criterion: If across 30 runs on ER(n=20, d=3, N=1000), CausalQD does NOT achieve significantly more MECs (p > 0.05, one-sided Wilcoxon signed-rank), the diversity advantage is not supported. If this fails AND Conjecture 2 fails AND ES Prediction 3 fails, CausalQD should be rescoped to a systems contribution with no diversity claims.

conjectures.name: Descriptor Non-Degeneracy
conjectures.statement: The MI-profile behavioral descriptor maps distinct MECs to distinct archive cells for n ≤ 30, N ≥ 500 under faithful linear Gaussian models. Specifically, the intra-MEC descriptor variance is < 50% of inter-MEC descriptor variance.
conjectures.motivation: RT and VC flag descriptor degeneracy as a kill criterion: if most high-scoring DAGs map to the same cell regardless of MEC membership, the archive provides no meaningful organization. AD's 13-dimensional descriptor is the best available design, but its non-degeneracy is an empirical property. Tested via ES's Prediction 5.
conjectures.falsification_criterion: If the intra/inter MEC variance ratio exceeds 0.8 (descriptors barely discriminate MECs), the descriptor design is inadequate. The project should then pivot to MEC-only descriptors (skeleton + v-structures), which are theoretically guaranteed to separate MECs but may not provide useful within-MEC diversity.

remarks.name: Operator Correctness
remarks.statement: Mutations respecting topological order preserve acyclicity: if G is a DAG with topological ordering π and edge (i,j) is added only when π(i) < π(j), the result is a DAG. Crossover with merged ordering preserves acyclicity by construction: edges are filtered to respect the merged topological order π_m, so no cycles can form.
remarks.demoted_reason: These are trivially true by construction — they restate the definition of topological ordering. All five experts agreed on demotion (FM: 'demote to Remark'; AD: 'trivially true'; RT: 'definition of topological ordering'; VC: 'correctness lemmas, not contributions').

remarks.name: Descriptor Dimension Lower Bound
remarks.label: rem:descriptor_dimension
remarks.statement: Any descriptor β : D_n → R^d that assigns distinct cells to all MECs under a uniform grid with K divisions per axis must satisfy K^d ≥ m_n, so d ≥ log(m_n)/log(K). For n=5: d ≥ 3 (13D sufficient). For n=10: d ≥ 7 (13D sufficient). For n=20: d ≥ 30 (13D provably insufficient). This quantifies where the 13-dimensional descriptor breaks down, complementing Limitation 9.
remarks.implication: For n > 15, the system's practical value relies on ε-faithful archive coverage of posterior-significant MECs (Definition D7), not full MEC separation.

remarks.name: Mixing Time Framework for Algorithm Comparison
remarks.label: rem:mixing_time_comparison
remarks.statement: To cover K archive cells, expected iteration counts are: Random walk O(|D_n|); Order MCMC O(K · τ_mix · n² log n) with τ_mix = O(n³ log n); CausalQD O(K log K / p_eff). CausalQD outperforms Order MCMC when p_eff ≳ log K / (τ_mix · n² log n) ∼ 1/(n⁵ log n). For n=10: threshold p_eff ≳ 2×10⁻⁶. For n=20: p_eff ≳ 3.5×10⁻⁸.
remarks.honest_assessment: No current theory establishes that CausalQD's effective generation probability exceeds this threshold. The claim in Conjecture 1 is empirical.

remarks.name: DAG Diameter and Non-Uniform Mutation
remarks.label: rem:diameter
remarks.statement: D_n has diameter C(n,2) = n(n-1)/2 under d_SHD. Reaching an arbitrary target requires Ω(n²) single-edge mutations. Theorem 7's lower bound is tight only under approximately uniform cell coverage; non-uniform visitation makes actual filling time much larger.

remarks.name: ESS Diagnostics and Leave-One-Out Stability
remarks.label: rem:loo_stability
remarks.statement: For archive BMA with M cells and weights {w_i}: (a) Effective diversity: ED ≥ M/r² where r = w_max/w_min ≤ exp(Δ_q/τ). (b) Leave-one-out stability: |μ̂_τ - μ̂_τ^(-i)| ≤ w_i·Δ_f/(1-w_i). If w_max ≤ 1/2, then |μ̂_τ - μ̂_τ^(-i)| ≤ Δ_f/(M-1).

remarks.name: Incremental CPDAG Updates
remarks.label: rem:lazy_mec
remarks.statement: When a single edge is modified, CPDAG changes only near the affected nodes in most cases. Incremental update heuristic locally re-applies Meek rules R1–R4 and updates XOR-based CPDAG hash. Worst-case remains O(n³) due to possible cascade. Fallback triggered in <2% of mutations for ER graphs with n≤30, d≤4.

algorithms.name: CausalMAP-Elites Main Loop
algorithms.description: Core quality-diversity optimization loop with three phases: warm-start initialization (10% budget), main QD loop with UCB1 operator selection (85% budget), and analytic certificate computation (5% budget).
detailed_pseudocode: grid_dims ∈ ℤ^d — grid resolution per descriptor dim
Output: A: Archive, R: dict — archive and certificates
// Phase 2: Main QD loop (85% of budget)
for t = 1 to T_main step batch_size:
// Phase 3: Certificate computation (5% of budget, analytic)
algorithms.complexity: Phase 0: O(N·n²). Phase 1: O(T_init · (n² + C_desc)) where C_desc = O(n² · d_max²). Phase 2: O(T_main · (n² + C_desc)). Phase 3: O(|A| · |E_avg| · d_max²). Total: O(T_budget · n² · d_max²) + O(N · n²). Space: O(|A| · n² + cache_size · n).
algorithms.key_changes_from_original: Added UCB1 operator selection (replacing unspecified adaptation), warm-start with GES-perturbed initialization, early termination on convergence, explicit budget allocation across phases.

algorithms.name: Archive Initialization
algorithms.description: Creates initial archive population with diverse density coverage (sparse, dense, GES-informed) rather than uniform random.
detailed_pseudocode: Input:  data D, sample covariance S, grid specification, init budget K
A ← empty Archive with grid from grid_spec
algorithms.complexity: O(K · (n² + C_desc + C_GES)) where C_GES = O(n² · d_max) per GES run with limited budget.

algorithms.name: Acyclicity-Preserving Mutation
algorithms.description: Modifies DAG structure while maintaining acyclicity through topological ordering constraints. Density-adaptive operator weights.
detailed_pseudocode: Input:  G = (V,E) as adjacency matrix adj ∈ {0,1}^{n×n}
Output: G' as modified adjacency matrix
if i ≠ j and adj'[i,j]==0 and topo_pos[i] < topo_pos[j]:
return adj'  // no valid addition found
elif not CAN-REACH(adj', j, i):  // O(n+m) BFS
algorithms.complexity: Add: expected O(n²/|E_missing|) = O(1) for sparse graphs. Remove: O(|E|). Reverse: O(|E|) + O(n+m) for reachability check. Amortized: O(n + m) per mutation.

algorithms.name: Order-Based Crossover
algorithms.description: Combines two parent DAGs by merging their topological orderings (OX crossover) and selecting edges that respect the merged order.
detailed_pseudocode: Input:  parent DAGs G₁, G₂ with topological orderings σ₁, σ₂
// OX crossover: random segment from σ₁, rest from σ₂
for each edge (i,j) in E₁ ∪ E₂:
// Edge respects merged order → candidate for inclusion
E' ← E' ∪ {(i,j)}     // single-parent: keep with prob 0.6
// By construction, G' is a DAG (all edges respect σ_m)
algorithms.complexity: O(|E₁| + |E₂|) for edge filtering against merged order. Cycle repair is unnecessary by construction — filtering by merged topological order guarantees acyclicity.

algorithms.name: Behavioral Descriptor Computation — Structural
algorithms.description: Computes 7-dimensional structural descriptor using efficient DP-based algorithms. Replaces exponential all-paths enumeration with O(n+m) longest path computation.
detailed_pseudocode: Input:  DAG G = (V, E) as adjacency matrix
longest_path ← LONGEST-PATH-DP(adj)     // O(n+m) via topo sort DP
algorithms.complexity: O(n + m + n · d_max²). Key fix: replaced exponential all-paths enumeration with O(n+m) longest-path DP.

algorithms.name: Behavioral Descriptor Computation — Information-Theoretic
algorithms.description: Computes 4 MI summary statistics plus 2 MEC features. Total descriptor dimension: 13 (7 structural + 4 MI + 2 MEC). Eliminates infeasible CI signature enumeration.
detailed_pseudocode: Input:  DAG G as adjacency matrix, S ∈ ℝ^{n×n} sample covariance
ρ ← PARTIAL-CORRELATION(i, j, pa_union, S)  // O(|pa|³)
cpdag ← DAG-TO-CPDAG(adj)                // O(n³) via Meek rules
n_directed ← count directed edges in cpdag
n_undirected ← count undirected edges in cpdag
algorithms.complexity: MI profile: O(n² · d_max³). MEC features: O(n³) for CPDAG via Meek rules. Total: O(n³ + n² · d_max³). For sparse graphs (d_max = O(1)): O(n³). Key change: eliminated CI signature enumeration (O(n⁵) for k_max=3) and replaced with 4 MI summary statistics.

algorithms.name: MEC Hash Computation
algorithms.description: Computes canonical hash of Markov equivalence class via CPDAG representation.
detailed_pseudocode: Output: canonical hash string of MEC [G]
cpdag ← DAG-TO-CPDAG(G)          // O(n³) via Meek rules
// Serialize CPDAG adjacency matrix to canonical string
// Two DAGs are in the same MEC iff their CPDAGs are identical
if cpdag has directed edge i→j: hash_str += '1'
elif cpdag has undirected edge i-j and i<j: hash_str += '2'
algorithms.complexity: O(n³) for CPDAG computation + O(n²) for serialization + O(n²) for SHA256. Total: O(n³). Note: Nauty-based canonical labeling is optional for additional efficiency but not required — CPDAG identity suffices for MEC comparison.

algorithms.name: Analytic Certificate Computation (F-Test Based)
algorithms.description: Computes edge and path certificates using the partial F-test framework (Theorem 2). Replaces computationally infeasible bootstrap (B=1000) with analytic computation. Uses F-test threshold instead of Lipschitz bound.
detailed_pseudocode: Input:  A — archive, D ∈ ℝ^{N×n} — data, S — sample covariance
// Compute BIC score difference (edge certificate value)
// Certificate: edge stable if p-value < γ
for path P = (e₁, …, e_k) in ENUMERATE-SHORT-PATHS(G, max_len=3):
path_p ← max(edge_certs[eᵢ].p_value for eᵢ in P) · k
algorithms.complexity: Per edge: O(d_max²) for residual variance + O(d_max³) for partial correlation. Per DAG: O(|E| · d_max³). Total: O(|A| · |E_avg| · d_max³). For |A|=500, |E_avg|=15, d_max=5: 500 × 15 × 125 = 937,500 operations — trivially fast (< 1 second). Eliminates the B=1000 bootstrap multiplier entirely (was: 7.5M BIC evaluations, ~50 minutes).
algorithms.key_changes_from_original: Replaced bootstrap resampling (B=1000, computationally infeasible) with analytic F-test certificate. Replaced Lipschitz bound (L_BIC = 10/N, a hardcoded magic number yielding vacuous certificates) with partial correlation-based variance (tight, distribution-derived). Added path certificates with Bonferroni correction (replacing incorrect independence assumption).

algorithms.name: Adaptive Cell Refinement
algorithms.description: Dynamically refines archive cells using a kd-tree over 13D descriptor space. Phase 1: standard archive update. Phase 2: if history buffer exceeds threshold and descriptor diameter > ε_b, split cell along maximum-variance dimension at median. Reinsert history into child cells.
detailed_pseudocode: Algorithm ADAPTIVE-CELL-REFINEMENT(A, ε_b, H, H_min, G', b', f'):
Input: A — archive (kd-tree), ε_b — diameter threshold, H — buffer size
if A(c) = ⊥ or f' > q(A(c)): A(c) ← G'
Append G' to H_c; evict oldest if |H_c| > H
SplitCell(A, c, d*, median); Reinsert H_c; Clear H_c
algorithms.complexity: Lookup: O(log K'). Split: O(H²n² + H log K'). Amortized: O(log K' + Hn²). Memory: O(K'·H·n²).
algorithms.key_changes_from_original: Uses kd-tree instead of uniform grid; adapts resolution based on observed descriptor diversity; preserves elites during splits (Lemma 4).

algorithms.name: Archive-Based Bayesian Model Averaging
algorithms.description: Deterministic BMA over archive DAGs using Boltzmann weights. Computes point estimate, posterior spread, effective diversity (reciprocal Herfindahl), and robustness certificate for any causal query.
detailed_pseudocode: Input: A — archive with M cells, D — data, τ — temperature, f — causal query
log Z ← log(Σ_j exp(w̃_j)); w_i ← exp(w̃_i - log Z)
Phase 2: Evaluate f_i ← f(G_{c_i}) for each cell
Phase 3: μ̂_τ ← Σ w_i f_i; σ̂²_τ ← Σ w_i(f_i - μ̂_τ)²
Phase 4: ED ← 1/Σ w_i²; δ_sign ← Σ_{sign(f_i)≠sign(μ̂_τ)} w_i
algorithms.key_changes_from_original: New algorithm. Uses temperature schedule (D9) and robustness certificate (D10).

complexity_analysis.summary: CausalQD's computational bottleneck is behavioral descriptor computation, not BIC scoring.

per_component_costs.BIC_scoring: O(d_max²) per local score with cached covariance. With score cache hit rate ~70%, amortized cost is O(0.3 · d_max²) per evaluation.
per_component_costs.mutation: O(n + m) amortized per mutation (see Algorithm 3).
per_component_costs.structural_descriptor: O(n + m + n · d_max²) per DAG (Algorithm 5).
per_component_costs.MI_descriptor: O(n² · d_max³) per DAG (Algorithm 6). This is the bottleneck for n > 10.

scalability_cutoffs.full_algorithm: n ≤ 20: all components feasible on laptop
scalability_cutoffs.skeleton_restricted: 20 < n ≤ 35: restrict MI computation to skeleton-adjacent pairs only. Reduces MI descriptor from O(n²·d_max³) to O(|skeleton|·d_max³).
scalability_cutoffs.not_recommended: n > 50: descriptor computation dominates even with skeleton restriction. Consider alternative methods (Order MCMC, NOTEARS).
scalability_cutoffs.CI_signature: n > 20: full CI signature (k_max=3) infeasible — O(n⁵) per DAG. Use MI summary statistics instead (Algorithm 6).

comparison_with_baselines.GES_single_run: O(n² · |E_max| · d_max) per run. For n=20: ~500 BIC evaluations, ~2 sec.
comparison_with_baselines.GES_30_restarts: 30 × GES cost. For n=20: ~15,000 evaluations, ~60 sec.
comparison_with_baselines.Order_MCMC: Precomputation: O(n · C(n-1,k) · k²). Per step: O(n · k). For n=20, k=3: precomputation ~160K ops (~1 sec), per step ~60 ops. 50K steps: ~3M ops + precomputation ≈ 10 sec total. Note: per-step cost is lower than CausalQD because Order MCMC does not compute descriptors.
comparison_with_baselines.note: CausalQD's overhead vs. baselines is the descriptor computation. This is the price of diversity-organized search.

decision_scenario.name: Robust Intervention Planning Under Structural Uncertainty
decision_scenario.description: A researcher observes n variables and wants to decide which variable to intervene on to affect a target. Under BIC scoring, multiple DAGs may score similarly but imply different optimal interventions. CausalQD's archive reveals which intervention recommendations are robust across plausible causal structures and which are fragile.
decision_scenario.metric: Intervention Regret = E[Y | do(a_method)] - E[Y | do(a*)], computed analytically for linear Gaussian SCMs.

falsifiable_predictions.name: MEC Discovery Advantage
falsifiable_predictions.prediction: CausalQD archive contains ≥ 1.5× as many distinct MECs as 30-restart GES under equal evaluation budget.
falsifiable_predictions.falsification: If across 30 runs on ER(n=20, d=3, N=1000), CausalQD does NOT achieve significantly more MECs (p > 0.05, one-sided Wilcoxon signed-rank), the diversity claim is falsified.
falsifiable_predictions.test: Wilcoxon signed-rank (paired, 30 seeds), one-sided, α=0.05 with Holm-Šidák correction

falsifiable_predictions.name: Best Member Accuracy
falsifiable_predictions.prediction: Best-SHD archive member has SHD no worse than GES + 2 (non-inferiority).
falsifiable_predictions.falsification: If 95% CI for SHD difference excludes non-inferiority margin of 2.
falsifiable_predictions.test: One-sided non-inferiority test using bootstrap CI

falsifiable_predictions.name: Intervention Robustness Advantage
falsifiable_predictions.prediction: Robust intervention selection from CausalQD archive achieves ≥ 20% lower intervention regret than single-DAG selection.
falsifiable_predictions.falsification: If regret reduction < 10% across all configurations, the decision-scenario value proposition collapses.
falsifiable_predictions.test: Paired Wilcoxon signed-rank, 30 replicates, effect size via rank-biserial

falsifiable_predictions.name: Certificate Non-Vacuity
falsifiable_predictions.prediction: For N ≥ 500 and n ≤ 20, at least 30% of true-positive edges receive F-test certificates (p < 0.05).
falsifiable_predictions.null_hypothesis: Fewer than 10% of true edges are certified
falsifiable_predictions.falsification: If at N=5000, n=10, fewer than 30% of true edges certified, certificates are vacuous as alleged.

falsifiable_predictions.name: Descriptor MEC Discrimination
falsifiable_predictions.prediction: Intra-MEC descriptor variance is < 50% of inter-MEC descriptor variance.
falsifiable_predictions.falsification: If variance ratio exceeds 0.8, descriptor design is inadequate.
falsifiable_predictions.test: Permutation test on within-vs-between MEC distances, 10,000 permutations

falsifiable_predictions.name: Negative Control on Trees
falsifiable_predictions.prediction: On tree-structured DAGs (unique MEC), CausalQD provides no diversity advantage.
falsifiable_predictions.null_hypothesis: CausalQD outperforms even on tree graphs
falsifiable_predictions.falsification: If CausalQD significantly outperforms on trees (p < 0.05), something unexpected is happening.

falsifiable_predictions.prediction: Archive BIC Gap Convergence Rate: For linear Gaussian SEMs with n=10, d=3 (ER graphs), the BIC score gap between the archive's best DAG and a ground-truth MEC representative decreases as O(log N / N). At N=500: Δ_BIC ≤ 10; N=1000: ≤ 5; N=5000: ≤ 1; N=10000: ≤ 0.5.
falsifiable_predictions.derived_from: Theorem 6 (Archive Score Consistency) and BIC consistency theory
falsifiable_predictions.null_hypothesis: The BIC gap does not decrease monotonically with N, or decreases slower than O(log N / N).
falsifiable_predictions.statistical_test: Fit Δ ~ c₁ log N / N and Δ ~ c₂ / √N using nonlinear least squares over 30 replicates per condition. Compare fits via AIC. Use Page's trend test (α = 0.05).
falsifiable_predictions.honest_caveat: The prediction conflates algorithmic convergence (archive search finding the best DAG) and statistical convergence (BIC identifying the true MEC).

falsifiable_predictions.prediction: Adaptive Cell Resolution Improves MEC Separation: An adaptive cell resolution strategy that splits cells when ≥ 2 distinct MECs are detected achieves ≥ 1.5× the number of MEC-pure cells compared to a fixed uniform grid of the same total cell count K.
falsifiable_predictions.derived_from: Algorithm 9 (Adaptive Cell Resolution, ACR) in Future Work
falsifiable_predictions.statistical_test: Paired Wilcoxon signed-rank test on MPI ratios across 30 replicates (α = 0.05, one-sided). Report Cohen's d.
falsifiable_predictions.honest_limitation: ACR's advantage depends on MEC boundaries aligning with coordinate axes in the 13-dimensional descriptor space. The 1.5× threshold is empirically motivated, not theoretically derived.

falsifiable_predictions.prediction: Archive-Guided Interventions Require Fewer Experiments: An archive-guided strategy for selecting intervention targets requires ≤ 50% as many single-variable interventions as a random intervention strategy to uniquely identify the true DAG G* (up to interventional equivalence).
falsifiable_predictions.derived_from: Proposition 5 (Greedy Submodularity) and Algorithm 10 (AGID) in Future Work
falsifiable_predictions.statistical_test: Two-sample Wilcoxon rank-sum test on intervention counts across 30 replicates per graph structure (α = 0.05, one-sided). Use 10 distinct graph structures.
falsifiable_predictions.connection: Optimal experimental design theory suggests O(log m) vs O(m) interventions; the 50% efficiency gain is a conservative operationalization.

falsifiable_predictions.prediction: Archive Calibration as Approximate Posterior: Archive covers ≥90% of posterior mass at N ≥ 5,000, with restricted KL divergence < 0.1 nats.
falsifiable_predictions.null_hypothesis: Coverage < 90%, or no monotone improvement with N.
falsifiable_predictions.statistical_test: One-sided Wilcoxon signed-rank against 0.9. Secondary: Page's trend test.
falsifiable_predictions.honest_caveat: Meaningful only if MCMC reference posterior has converged. Unreliable for n ≥ 15.

falsifiable_predictions.prediction: Transfer Across Sub-Gaussian Noise: ρ_transfer = |MEC_alt|/|MEC_Gauss| ≥ 0.8 for Uniform and Laplace noise.
falsifiable_predictions.null_hypothesis: ρ_transfer < 0.8 for at least one sub-Gaussian type.
falsifiable_predictions.protocol: 30 ER graphs, n ∈ {10, 20}, d=3, N=1000. Student-t₅ excluded (13% power).
falsifiable_predictions.honest_caveat: Student-t₅ reported as exploratory only due to insufficient power.

falsifiable_predictions.prediction: Certificate Ordering Consistency: Spearman ρ_S ≥ 0.7 between certificate strength and total causal effect magnitude over all true edges.
falsifiable_predictions.statistical_test: Wilcoxon signed-rank against 0.7, one-sided. Supplementary: Kendall's τ_b.
falsifiable_predictions.honest_caveat: Increased r from 30 to 40 for 89% power under Holm correction (74% at r=30).

falsifiable_predictions.prediction: Archive Fill Rate Scaling Law: F(T) = c·T^α with α ∈ [0.3, 0.7], power-law fit via Clauset–Shalizi–Newman procedure.
falsifiable_predictions.null_hypothesis: Fill rate not power-law (KS p < 0.1), or α ∉ [0.3, 0.7].
falsifiable_predictions.statistical_test: KS goodness-of-fit + Vuong LRT. Secondary: log-log R² ≥ 0.95.
falsifiable_predictions.protocol: 30 ER graphs, n ∈ {10, 20, 30}, d=3, N=1000, T=10⁶, 13 log-spaced checkpoints.
falsifiable_predictions.honest_caveat: Non-standard power analysis; simulation-based validation confirms >90% acceptance.

baselines.name: GES with 30 Random Restarts
baselines.implementation: causal-learn ges() with random ER(p=0.3) initial DAGs

baselines.name: Order MCMC
baselines.tests: diversity from Bayesian posterior vs. QD archive
baselines.implementation: Friedman & Koller (2003) Algorithm 1, validated against BiDAG
baselines.budget_matching: Equal total MCMC steps = total CausalQD evaluations
baselines.mixing_time: O(n³ log n) for uniform chain; O(n³ log² n) posterior-weighted. For n=20: ~24,000 steps to mix.

baselines.name: Stability Selection
baselines.budget_matching: 100 × GES evaluations per subsample = total budget

baselines.name: PC Algorithm at Multiple α Levels
CD-NOD
baselines.citation: Zhang et al. (2017). Causal discovery from nonstationary/heterogeneous data: Skeleton estimation and orientation determination. IJCAI.
baselines.rationale: Current baselines assume i.i.d. data from a single distribution (Assumption S1). CD-NOD exploits distributional shifts across domains/conditions, leveraging strictly more information than CausalQD.

protocol.input: Same data as other baselines, with condition labels (for Sachs) or artificially generated shifts (5 environments with intervention-induced shifts on 20% of variables for synthetic).
protocol.budget_matching: Match by total sample usage (N_total = N × 5 across environments for synthetic; use all 14 conditions for Sachs).
protocol.metrics: MEC count, SHD to ground truth, intervention regret.

baselines.hypothesis: CD-NOD should outperform CausalQD on SHD (it uses more information). CausalQD should match or exceed CD-NOD on MEC diversity (CD-NOD returns a single graph, not an archive).

baselines.name: IDA
baselines.full_name: Intervention calculus when DAG is Absent
baselines.citation: Maathuis et al. (2009). Estimating high-dimensional intervention effects from observational data. Annals of Statistics, 37(6A):3133–3164.
baselines.rationale: IDA estimates causal effects E[Y | do(X_i := x)] directly from observational data by considering all DAGs in the MEC of the PC-estimated CPDAG. Most direct competitor to CausalQD's intervention robustness (Definition D6).

protocol.procedure: Run PC algorithm at α = 0.01 to estimate CPDAG. Apply IDA to estimate multiset of possible causal effects for each (X_i, X_j) pair.
protocol.metrics: Effect estimation accuracy, intervention selection accuracy, MEC coverage (IDA covers exactly one MEC by construction).

baselines.hypothesis: CausalQD should provide wider but similarly calibrated effect bounds (it covers multiple MECs). If CausalQD's bounds are wider but no better calibrated than IDA's, the archive adds complexity without benefit.

baselines.name: DAGMA
baselines.rationale: Continuous optimization via log-determinant acyclicity constraint on M-matrices. Avoids gradient saturation unlike NOTEARS.
baselines.protocol: 30 random restarts, λ₁ ∈ {0.01, 0.03, 0.05}, BIC model selection. Wall-clock matched.
baselines.hypothesis: H_{8a}: CausalQD ≥1.5× MECs vs DAGMA. H_{8b}: DAGMA SHD within +2 of CausalQD.

baselines.name: GOLEM
baselines.rationale: Likelihood-based optimization with soft sparsity. Avoids hard acyclicity constraints.
baselines.protocol: 30 restarts, λ₁ ∈ {0.01, 0.02, 0.05}, λ₂ ∈ {1, 5, 10}. Wall-clock matched. Threshold |w_{ij}| > 0.3.
baselines.hypothesis: H_{9a}: GOLEM SHD within +2 of CausalQD. H_{9b}: CausalQD ≥1.5× MEC diversity.

baselines.name: DiBS
baselines.rationale: Differentiable Bayesian structure learning via SVGD with 100 particles. Most direct Bayesian competitor.
baselines.protocol: 100 particles, 5000 SVGD iterations, linear Gaussian likelihood. Budget matched by gradient evaluations.
baselines.hypothesis: H_{10a}: DiBS may match CausalQD diversity. H_{10b}: DiBS may have better calibration. H_{10c}: CausalQD better best-DAG SHD.

baselines.name: MCMC-BMA
baselines.rationale: Structure MCMC with single-edge MH proposals. Gold-standard Bayesian baseline with known mixing challenges.
baselines.protocol: 10⁶ iterations, 4 chains, 20% burn-in, thin every 100. Convergence: R̂ < 1.05.
baselines.hypothesis: H_{11a}: CausalQD discovers MECs MCMC misses. H_{11b}: MCMC better calibrated for n=5. H_{11c}: CausalQD faster fill rate.

ablations.name: CausalQD-NoIllum
ablations.removes: Archive organization → keep top-k elite population
ablations.tests: Is illumination (archive) necessary vs. simple elite population?

ablations.name: CausalQD-MutOnly
ablations.tests: Does crossover actually help, or is mutation sufficient?

ablations.name: CausalQD-RandDesc
ablations.tests: Do information-theoretic descriptors matter vs. random?

ablations.name: CausalQD-StructOnly
ablations.removes: MI descriptors → structural descriptors only (edge count, degree)
ablations.tests: Are MI-profile descriptors better than simple structural ones?

synthetic.data_model: Linear Gaussian SCM: Xᵢ = Σ_{j∈Pa(i)} β_ji Xⱼ + εᵢ, εᵢ ~ N(0, σ²)
synthetic.graph_generation: ER DAGs with edge probability = expected_degree/(n-1)
synthetic.configurations_P1: n ∈ {10, 20}, degree = 3, N = 1000, coeff = faithful. 30 replicates × 5 methods.
synthetic.configurations_P2: n=5 exhaustive + n=20 N=5000 + adversarial coefficients.

negative_controls: Tree-structured DAGs (unique MEC) — CausalQD should NOT outperform
Very large N (N=50,000) with n=10 — diversity should shrink as BIC concentrates
n=5 exhaustive enumeration — CausalQD should find a subset of all MECs
Random (shuffled) data — no causal structure; diversity meaningless

budget_analysis.P1_core: 2 configs × 30 reps × 5 methods × ~10 min = 50 hrs single-core / 12.5 hrs 4-core
budget_analysis.total: ~37.5 hrs on 4-core laptop (MacBook Pro M2 or equivalent)
budget_analysis.hardware: Single 4-core laptop, 16 GB RAM, Python 3.9+ with NumPy, SciPy, NetworkX, causal-learn

metrics.QD_score: Σ_{occupied cells} q_normalized(A(c)), where q_normalized = (BIC - BIC_min) / (BIC_max - BIC_min)
metrics.QWMD: Σ_{M ∈ MECs(A)} max_{G ∈ M ∩ A} q_normalized(G) — quality-weighted MEC diversity
metrics.SHD_diversity: median pairwise SHD across archive (with quality constraint)
metrics.certificate_calibration: ECE via 5-fold cross-validation of edge stability

power_analysis.description: Power analysis for all nine predictions, computing minimum replicates r needed to detect hypothesized effect size at 80% power with α = 0.05.
power_analysis.framework: For Wilcoxon signed-rank test (Predictions 1, 3, 5, 6, 8), ARE relative to paired t-test is 3/π ≈ 0.955. r = ⌈(z_α + z_β)² / (d² · ARE)⌉ where z_α = 1.645 (one-sided), z_β = 0.842 (80% power).

predictions.note: Underpowered at r=30 (13% power at d=0.2). Increased to r=50 (32% power at d=0.2, 97% power at d=0.5) as compromise.
Power exceeds 99% at r=30 under Holm correction.
87% power under Holm at r=30. Student-t₅ excluded (13% power).
Increased to r=40 (89% power). At r=30, power is only 74%.
Non-standard power analysis. Simulation-based: >90% acceptance for α ∈ [0.3, 0.7].

total_computational_budget.mitigation: Run Predictions 7–9 only on n ∈ {5, 10, 20} (drop n=50) and reduce CD-NOD to synthetic-only. Reduces additional compute to ~50 hours.
total_computational_budget.statistical_correction: Holm-Bonferroni for 13 predictions at family-wise α=0.05

novelty_claims.claim: Applying MAP-Elites (QD optimization) to causal DAG structure learning
novelty_claims.label: genuinely_new
novelty_claims.justification: No prior work applies QD illumination to causal discovery. The combination of archive-based search with causal-aware behavioral descriptors is novel as a system.

novelty_claims.claim: MI-profile behavioral descriptors for organizing DAG space
novelty_claims.label: new_combination
novelty_claims.justification: MI profiles are known in information theory; using them as behavioral descriptors for MAP-Elites is a new combination. The simplified 13-dimensional descriptor (7 structural + 4 MI summary + 2 MEC features) is new.

novelty_claims.claim: Finite-sample MEC separation bound via MI-profile descriptors (Theorem 1)
novelty_claims.label: new_combination
novelty_claims.justification: Combines Verma & Pearl's MEC characterization (known, 1990) with finite-sample concentration for MI estimation (known) in the QD descriptor context (new). The sample complexity N = Ω(α⁻² log n) is new in this setting.

novelty_claims.claim: Connecting BIC edge certificates to partial F-tests (Theorem 2)
novelty_claims.label: known_in_new_context
novelty_claims.justification: Partial F-tests for edge significance are textbook regression. The contribution is showing that the 'novel' Lipschitz certificate reduces to this well-understood test, and providing tight variance formulas via the delta method.

novelty_claims.claim: Decision-theoretic value of diverse archives for intervention planning (Theorem 4)
novelty_claims.label: new_combination
novelty_claims.justification: Worst-case aggregation bounds are elementary probability. The contribution is connecting QD archive diversity to intervention accuracy — providing the 'so what' for the framework.

novelty_claims.claim: Intervention robustness metric R(a) for comparing actions across archive
novelty_claims.label: genuinely_new
novelty_claims.justification: The specific metric R(a) combining Boltzmann-weighted archive averaging with intervention decision quality is new. Related to Bayesian model averaging but applied to QD archives rather than posterior samples.

novelty_claims.label: new_combination
novelty_claims.justification: Eliminating bootstrap in favor of analytic F-test certificates is a practical contribution. The underlying statistics are known; the algorithmic specification for QD archives is new.

novelty_claims.claim: Coverage bound exposing vacuity for n > 15 (Proposition 1)
novelty_claims.label: known_in_new_context
novelty_claims.justification: Coupon-collector bounds are textbook. The contribution is honestly applying them to expose that coverage guarantees are physically meaningless for realistic problem sizes.

risk_mitigations.risk: CausalQD finds many DAGs but few distinct MECs (RT's fundamental objection)
risk_mitigations.mitigation: Tested via ES Predictions 1 and 3. If CausalQD doesn't find more MECs than 30-restart GES AND doesn't reduce intervention regret, the project should be rescoped to a systems contribution.
risk_mitigations.gate_condition: If both Predictions 1 and 3 fail, diversity claims are dropped.

risk_mitigations.risk: Descriptor space degenerates (most DAGs map to same cell)
risk_mitigations.mitigation: Tested via ES Prediction 5 and Conjecture 2. If intra/inter MEC variance ratio > 0.8, pivot to MEC-only descriptors (skeleton + v-structures).
risk_mitigations.gate_condition: If Prediction 5 fails, descriptor redesign is required before publishing.

risk_mitigations.risk: Certificates are vacuous at practical sample sizes
risk_mitigations.mitigation: Tested via ES Prediction 4. The F-test certificates (Theorem 2) are much tighter than the original Lipschitz bounds. If still vacuous at N=5000, drop certificate contribution from paper.
risk_mitigations.gate_condition: If Prediction 4 fails at N=5000, certificates are a known limitation, not a contribution.

risk_mitigations.risk: Order MCMC achieves similar diversity with better quality
risk_mitigations.mitigation: AD provides mixing time analysis for fair budget matching. ES's experimental design includes Order MCMC as primary baseline. If Order MCMC dominates, the paper acknowledges this honestly.
risk_mitigations.gate_condition: If Order MCMC strictly dominates CausalQD on all metrics, the theoretical framing must change to 'we studied QD for causal discovery and found it does not outperform Bayesian methods.'

risk_mitigations.risk: Strong faithfulness violations in real data
risk_mitigations.mitigation: Include adversarial coefficient regime in synthetic experiments. Report Theorem 1 bounds with estimated α from data. Acknowledge that for small α, all guarantees degrade.
risk_mitigations.gate_condition: None — this is an inherent limitation of the framework, not a testable risk.

risk_mitigations.mitigation: AD's scalability analysis provides honest cutoffs. Algorithm 6 uses MI summaries instead of CI signatures. Skeleton restriction for 20 < n ≤ 35. Do not claim applicability for n > 50.
risk_mitigations.gate_condition: If n=20 experiments cannot complete within 1 hour on laptop, reduce grid resolution or descriptor dimension.

risk_mitigations.mitigation: Theorem 3 provides dimension bounds. With d=13 (simplified descriptor), PCA may not be needed. Test empirically whether full 13-dim descriptor suffices or PCA is required.
risk_mitigations.gate_condition: If MEC collision rate > 20% after PCA, use full descriptors (feasible for d=13).

risk_mitigations.mitigation: Ablation CausalQD-MutOnly tests whether crossover adds value. If mutation-only performs equally, simplify the algorithm.
risk_mitigations.gate_condition: None — crossover value is an empirical question.

risk_mitigations.risk: Theory is rejected as 'just applying known techniques in a new context'
risk_mitigations.mitigation: Honest novelty labeling (each claim labeled as genuinely_new / new_combination / known_in_new_context). Position paper at systems/ML venue, not theory venue. The theory section supports the empirical contribution rather than standing alone.
risk_mitigations.gate_condition: None — this is a presentation/positioning decision.

limitations.name: Coverage guarantees are vacuous for n > 15
limitations.description: The coupon-collector bound (Proposition 1) requires T > 10³⁸⁰ iterations for n=20 with p_flip=0.01 — exceeding the number of operations possible in the observable universe (~10¹²⁰). CausalQD's practical success depends on problem structure (informed initialization, score-guided search, sparsity), not worst-case theory. All experts agree this is an inherent limitation of worst-case analysis in super-exponential spaces.
limitations.source: All experts (FM, AD, ES, RT, VC)

limitations.name: Certificates are only meaningful under Gaussian assumptions
limitations.description: The partial F-test certificate (Theorem 2) requires the linear Gaussian SEM assumption.
